segments.s638-g89: The pattern 'یکی ... و یکی ...' is a distributive construction meaning 'one ... and another ...'. Here each 'یکی' ('one') is followed by the preposition 'سوی' meaning 'toward' (the shorter poetic form without the usual 'به'). 'خشک' ('dry') stands for 'dry land/shore' by substantive use of an adjective in poetic diction, and 'آب' is the noun 'water'. The conjunction 'و' links the two parallel directions.
segments.s638-g90: 'برفتند' is the simple past, 3rd person plural ('they went/they departed'); the prefix 'بر-' is a classical/poetic variant attached to رفتند. 'شادان‌دل' is a compounded adjective/adverbial phrase meaning 'joyful of heart' (poetic order: adjective+noun), and 'پرشتاب' is another adjective used adverbially ('full of haste' / 'hurried'). The two manner-phrases are joined by 'و' and describe how they departed.
segments.s638-g73: برآورد is a past third-person singular verb meaning 'brought forth' or 'disclosed' (here an accomplished action). The order is verb–subject–object in Persian poetry: برآورد (he disclosed) + بهرام (Bahram, subject) + راز از نهفت (the secret from concealment). راز is the direct object and از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from hiding/concealment' modifying راز.
segments.s638-g74: سخنهای ایرانیان is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the words/utterances of the Iranians' (سخنهای is the plural with the ezāfe sense implied), with را omitted here because poetic style allows direct predication; باز is an adverb 'again', and گفت is the past verb 'said/spoke' referring back to Bahram. Word order places the object phrase before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s638-g75: که here introduces an indirect question/relative content clause '(that) what/ how ...', functioning like 'what' or 'how'. کردار 'deed, conduct' is the topic, followed by چون بود 'was how' (چون = 'how/what kind of', بود = past 'was'). و links to اندیشه 'thought', followed again by the interrogative/adverbial چون 'how'. The construction lists two parallel items (کردار ... و اندیشه ...), both queried about their nature.
segments.s638-g76: که opens a continuation of the indirect statement 'and how ...'. بودم is the first‑person past 'I was'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'به + این' (here split in the segmentation as ب + دین) meaning 'to/into this' or 'by this', so بدین داستان = 'into/through this tale'. رهنمون is a predicative noun/participle here meaning 'guided/led' (literally 'guide'), so the clause as a whole means 'and how I was guided into this story.'
segments.s638-g77: می‌چند is a verb form here meaning a small action of plucking/collecting or taking a little (the progressive prefix می‑ appears but the main verb context is past), paired with خوردند 'they ate/took' (past plural), so the phrase conveys 'they took/ate a little'. و برخاستند 'and they rose up' is a simple past clause following it; the two actions are sequential.
segments.s638-g78: زبان را uses زبان ('tongue') with the definite/object marker را to mean 'their speech' or 'the tongue' as the object. به پوزش is a prepositional phrase 'with/into apology' (پوزش = 'apology'); بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/embellished' (here idiomatically 'they clothed/tempered their speech with apologies' — i.e. they began to speak apologetically). The verb bi‑آراستن carries the sense of making ready/embellishing.
segments.s638-g79: دو شاه is 'two kings'. دلارای is an epithet 'dolarāy' meaning 'beloved' or 'charming' modifying شاه; یزدان‌پرست is another epithet 'God‑worshipping/pious', also qualifying the kings. The sequence is a string of modifiers after the noun in a poetic appositional style.
segments.s638-g80: وفا را places وفا ('fidelity, loyalty') with the object marker را. بسودند is a past plural verb 'they bestowed/turned to good' (here 'they pledged' or 'gave' loyalty). بر دست دست is an idiomatic reduplicated phrase 'hand to hand' (بر = 'upon/toward', دست = 'hand'), indicating a mutual or physical pledge (like clasping hands). Word order places the object before the verb, as is normal.
segments.s638-g41: جفا (object) comes first for emphasis, followed by the past 2nd-person verb برگزیدی (‘you chose’). به جای is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in place of/instead of,’ and وفا is the noun ‘fidelity/loyalty’ that is being replaced. The clause literally reads ‘You chose cruelty instead of fidelity.’
segments.s638-g42: وفا را uses the object marker را to topicalize/mark ‘fidelity’ as the thing under discussion. پسندی is present tense 2nd-person singular ‘you prefer/like’; the interrogative کی here is rhetorical (‘when/how’ in effect) raising a reproachful question, and سزا is ‘desert/what is due’ (i.e. deserved reward or punishment). The clause asks rhetorically how/when one could prefer cruelty as the due for fidelity.
segments.s638-g43: چه گویم is a rhetorical phrase ‘what shall I say’ (چه introducing an exclamatory/question), with گویم here functioning as the verb ‘I say/shall I say’. تراکانک is a compacted/poetic element attached to ترا (to you/your) and shades the clause as directed at the addressee (here glossed as a fixed phrase in the line); فرزند بود is a simple past copula ‘was a son/child’. Overall: ‘What shall I say — that he was your son?’
segments.s638-g44: به اندیشه من is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in my thought / in my judgment’ (به + noun + possessive). خردمند بود is the predicate ‘he was wise’ with بود as the past copula. The word order is topic (به اندیشه من) then comment (خردمند بود).
segments.s638-g45: کنون ‘now’ sets the temporal frame. چون here functions as a subordinating/comparative particle ‘as/now that’, linking to the following predicate. دلاور سواری is a nominal phrase ‘valiant/heroic horseman’; شدست is an archaic/perfect-form verb meaning ‘has become’ (modern شده‌است). The clause means ‘now that he has become a valiant horseman.’
segments.s638-g46: گمانم is a noun with 1st-person possessive sense ‘my belief / I suppose’ functioning as the matrix of the sentence; که introduces the subordinate clause. شهریاری شدست uses شهریاری ‘kingship/rule’ as the predicate nominal and the archaic شدست ‘has become’ as the verb: ‘I suppose that he has become a ruler.’
segments.s638-g47: دل پارسی باوفا is the noun phrase ‘a Persian heart (that is) faithful’; the adjective باوفا follows the noun. کی بود is the interrogative past ‘when was…?’ — a rhetorical question asking when such faithfulness ever occurred. Word order places the temporal/interrogative particle after the nominal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s638-g48: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ‘when/if’. آری کند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form ‘he/it gives assent/says “yes”’ (کند = does/makes). رای او ‘his counsel/opinion’ is the possessed noun phrase; نی is the old poetic negation ‘not/no’, and بود is the past copula. The clause therefore says roughly ‘when he consents, his counsel is not/does not endure’ — a poetic way to say the counsel is not steady or reliable.
segments.s638-g81: کزین پس (literally ‘from this after’) is a poetic adverbial phrase meaning ‘henceforth/from now on.’ دل is the direct object/topic 'heart(s)'. نشکنیم is the present-subjunctive/optative 1st-person plural with the negative prefix نـ, here expressing a firm resolve (‘we will not break/turn away’); از راستی is a prepositional phrase ‘from/away from truth’ modifying دل.
segments.s638-g82: همی is an emphatic/frequentative adverb used in classical Persian to mark continuing intention (‘will/ever’). بیخ کژی is the direct object phrase ‘the root of crookedness’ (بیخ = root, کژی = crookedness); ز بن is a reinforcing prepositional phrase ‘from the foundation/root’ (poetic repetition for emphasis). برکنیم is the 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative meaning ‘let us remove/uproot.’
segments.s638-g83: وفادار باشیم is an exhortation in the present-subjunctive 1st-person plural (‘let us be faithful’). تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that' rather than temporal 'until'), and جاودان is an adverb/adjectival complement meaning ‘forever/eternal’ modifying the following clause (i.e., the duration of the hearing).
segments.s638-g84: سخن بشنویم is a purpose clause with the verb in the subjunctive 1st-person plural ‘may we hear (words).’ از لب introduces the source ('from the lip(s)'); بخردان is the plural/oblique of بخرد ‘the wise’, so از لب بخردان = 'from the lips of the wise.' Word order is source (از لب) + possessor (بخردان) after the noun.
segments.s638-g85: سپینود را is the definite object marked with را. نیز = 'also/too' modifies the whole action. پدرود کردن is a light-verb collocation meaning 'to bid farewell'; کرد is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'he did/bade.' The word order is object – (adverb) – verb (typical Persian SOV with postverbal verb).
segments.s638-g86: بر خویش تار = 'warp upon himself' where خویش (here split into خوی + ش) functions as a reflexive/possessive 'his own' (i.e. upon himself). و links the two objects (تار and پود). برش پود is best read as 'and the weft upon that/upon himself' and کرد is the past tense 'he set/made.' The phrase uses weaving terms (تار = warp, پود = weft) metaphorically but literally indicates he took/put on his fabric/gear.
segments.s638-g87: سبک‌پشت is a compound adjective meaning 'light-backed' or 'fickle/frivolous in fidelity' (here 'light' in the sense of not serious). بر یکدگر گاشتند is a past-tense verb phrase ('they placed/turned upon one another') with یکدگر = 'one another' and گاشتند = 'they put/placed/laid' — together meaning 'they turned their backs on each other.'
segments.s638-g88: دل کینه is a noun phrase 'heart of hatred' (i.e. 'hatred in the heart'). بر جای بگذاشتند is the verb phrase 'they left/they placed in place' (بگذاشتند = 'they left/placed'), so the whole clause means 'they left hatred in their hearts' or 'hatred remained in their hearts.'
segments.s638-g57: This is a nominal predicate followed by the emphatic copula 'منم' at the end. 'شهنشاه ایران و توران' names the title and its domain ('Shahanshah of Iran and Turan'); in poetry the ezafe is omitted and the whole phrase precedes the copula. 'منم' is the fused first‑person copula/pronoun ('I am'), placed sentence‑finally for emphasis.
segments.s638-g58: 'سپهدار و پشت دلیران' are two coordinated predicates joined by 'و', followed again by the copula 'منم'. Here 'پشت' is used metaphorically as 'support/backing' (not merely physical back), and 'دلیران' is the plural 'the brave/warriors'. The structure is title(s) + verb ('I am'), with the repeated 'منم' reinforcing the speaker's identity and roles.
segments.s638-g59: 'ازین پس' is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'from now on' (از + این/orthographic ین + پس). The phrase 'سزای تو نیکی' is the object ('a kindly recompense for you') placed before the verb, and 'کنم' is the first‑person present/subjunctive used here with a future/intent meaning ('I will do / I will render'). Word order is object–verb (typical in Persian).
segments.s638-g60: 'سر بدسگالت' = 'the head of your ill‑omened one' where '-ت' marks the second‑person possessor; 'بدسگال' is an epithet meaning 'ill‑omened/bad‑fated'. 'ز تن' uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from', and 'برکنم' (verb at the end) literally means 'I will pluck off/remove' — here 'to decapitate'. The verb is in the same present/subjunctive form used to express intention.
segments.s638-g61: 'به ایران' marks the place/realm ('to/in Iran'); 'به جای پدر' means 'in the stead/place of your father' (the preposition 'به' + 'جای' = 'in place of'). 'دارمت' is the colloquial/poetic contraction of 'دارم' + object enclitic 'ت' = 'I have/keep you' (first person verb with second person object). The sequence places locative/role phrases before the verb, as usual in Persian.
segments.s638-g62: 'هم' = 'also/likewise' introduces the clause. 'از باژ کشور' = 'on account of / because of the noise/clamor of the realm' ('از' = 'because of' here, 'باژ' = uproar/noise, 'کشور' = realm). 'نیازارمت' is a prefixed negative form built on 'آزار' with first‑person ending and second‑person object enclitic: ن + آزار + م + ت = 'I will not harm/trouble you'. The verb comes at clause end as usual.
segments.s638-g63: 'همان' is a demonstrative intensifier ('that very' / 'the same'); 'دخترت' is 'your daughter' (دختر + possessive enclitic 'ت'). 'شمع خاور' is a poetic epithet 'lamp/candle of the East' (metaphorical praise). The clause ends with 'بود' (past copula 'was'), so this is a past descriptive statement.
segments.s638-g64: 'سر بانوان را' = 'the heads of the ladies' with 'را' marking the definite/direct object/topic. 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as'), and 'افسر' here means 'helmet' (a head‑ornament); the copula 'بود' at the end gives 'was like a helmet'. The whole phrase says she adorned/crowned the women's heads (metaphorical praise).
segments.s638-g65: ز is the preposition 'from' placed before its noun phrase. گفتار او is a genitive phrase 'his speech' with the pronoun او following the noun. ماند is the perfect/past of ماندن 'remained, was left' and appears before its nominal complement (شنگل شگفت), so the verb precedes the noun phrase here (typical flexible word order in classical Persian). شنگل is an obscure/rare noun and شگفت functions here as a noun ('astonishment, wonder') describing that state.
segments.s638-g66: ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the head' (ز + noun). شاره هندوی is a noun + adjective/attributive phrase where شاره (an archaic term, likely a head‑ornament) is modified by هندوی 'Indian/Hindu' placed after it. برگرفت is the past tense compound verb (بر‑گرفت) 'took up/raised' and completes the clause; the actor is implicit from prior context.
segments.s638-g67: بزد is the past tense transitive 'he struck/struck at'. اسپ is its direct object 'steed/horse' placed after the verb. وز پیش functions as an adverbial/locative phrase; پیش here means 'in the presence of' and چندان سپاه is a noun phrase 'so great an army' with the quantifier/changer چندان preceding the noun سپاه.
segments.s638-g68: بیامد is the intransitive past 'he came/approached'. This verb takes two sequential prepositional complements here: به پوزش 'for/to seek pardon' (به marking purpose/goal) and به نزدیک شاه 'toward/near the king' (به marking direction/location); both به‑phrases are separate adjuncts attached to the verb.
segments.s638-g69: شهنشاه را uses the accusative/definite marker را after the noun 'the emperor' to mark the direct object. شاد is an adjective 'glad/joyful' describing the manner, and در بر گرفت is an idiomatic compound verb 'to take into one's embrace' (در + بر + گرفت) with گرفت as the past tense main verb.
segments.s638-g70: وزان is a connective 'and from that/thereupon' referring back to the preceding words. گفتها is the plural noun 'utterances/words' (object of the preposition), پوزش 'pardon/apology' is the thing taken or accepted, اندر is a locative/inside particle 'in/within' used here in the idiom 'پوزش اندر گرفتن' ('to take/accept pardon'), and گرفت is the past of گرفتن 'took/accepted'.
segments.s638-g71: به دیدار بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight/meeting of Bahram' (به + noun + genitive/name). شد is the copular/predicative past 'became' and links to the predicate adjective شادکام 'joyful/fortunate' which follows the verb as its complement.
segments.s638-g72: بیاراست is a perfective/past verb 'he adorned/prepared' (here used with خوان 'the table/banquet' as its object). The conjunction و links this verb to the next clause; بیاورد جام is a coordinated verb phrase 'he brought a cup' with بیاورد as the past/imperative form meaning 'brought/produce' and جام the object 'cup'. Both verbs share the same subject (he).
segments.s638-g1: سواری is the subject 'a horseman/rider'. The prepositional phrase ز قنوج تازان ('from Kannauj [the] raiders') follows to give origin/association, and the verb برفت (literary past) comes at the end: subject + origin phrase + past verb. This is a compact, archaic/poetic word order where the verb is final.
segments.s638-g2: به آگاهی رفتن is an infinitival/purpose phrase 'to go to give information / to inform'; رفتن is the infinitive 'to go'. شاه تفت is the recipient/title (the king named Tft/Taft) placed after the verbal phrase; in Persian the infinitive phrase expresses purpose. Note تفت as entered here doesn't match the listed lexical senses (so it's treated as a name/epithet).
segments.s638-g3: که is the subordinating/relative particle 'that' introducing reported information. برزوی و ایرانیان are coordinated subjects 'Barzū and the Iranians' and رفته‌اند is the perfect/pluperfect verbal form 'have gone/left' agreeing with a plural subject. The verb appears at the end, as is normal in literary Persian.
segments.s638-g4: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very'. دختر شاه را is a definite direct object ('the king's daughter'), with را marking the object. برده‌اند is the compound perfect 'have taken/carried off' (third‑person plural) describing the completed action.
segments.s638-g5: شنید is a simple past 'heard'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/news'. شنگل is the (proper) name of the hearer, and از نیک‌خواه marks the source 'from Nik‑khāh / from a well‑wisher'. The clause order here is verb + object + source, a common word order in poetic prose.
segments.s638-g6: چو functions here as a simile/conjunction 'as/like', introducing a comparison. آتش بیامد literally 'fire came' — a figurative expression meaning he burst forth like a blaze; بیامد is the past 'came'. ز نخچیرگاه is a source phrase 'from the hunting‑ground'.
segments.s638-g7: همه لشکر خویش را means 'all his army/host' with خویش as the possessive 'his' following the noun; را marks the whole phrase as the definite object. برنشاند is a causative/past-form verb 'set forth/deployed/placed' used here in the sense of mustering or arranging the troops.
segments.s638-g8: پس is a temporal/conclusive adverb 'then'. شاه بهرام 'King Bahram' is the subject and لشکر is the object; براند (from راندن) is a past/continuative sense 'drove/led' — here 'led the army' or 'set it in motion'. The verb appears after subject and object in this compact poetic clause.
segments.s638-g9: بدین‌گونه is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'in this manner' and opens the clause. تا ... رسید here uses تا in the spatial/extent sense 'as far as/up to' (not purpose). پیش دریا is a prepositional phrase 'in front of/the verge of the sea' and رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived' placed after the place phrase, a normal Persian SOV order in prose/poetry.
segments.s638-g10: This is a coordinated clause: سپینود is the subject name; و links the two names/clauses; بهرام یل را is a noun phrase with یل 'hero' as an epithet and را marking the definite object/emphatic NP; بدید is the past tense 'saw'. Word order and the object-marker را place emphasis on Bahram as the seen figure.
segments.s638-g11: غمی گشت uses غم + گشت (past of گشتن) to mean 'sorrow arose/there came sorrow' (گشت = 'became'/'became manifest'). بگذاشت here is a causative/predicative use 'put/caused' (literally 'set') and takes دریا 'the sea' as its object; به خشم 'into anger' uses به + noun to indicate change into a state.
segments.s638-g12: ازان سوی دریا is a frozen prepositional phrase 'from that side of the sea' (ازان = 'from that/thereupon', سوی = 'side/direction'). چو here means 'when', and بر + کردن with چشم (بر کرد چشم) is an idiomatic verb phrase 'to turn one's gaze/take a look toward' where بر marks the direction and کرد is the past 'did/turned'.
segments.s638-g13: بدیدش is the past tense 'saw' (بدید) with the enclitic -ش 'him/it' marking the direct object on the verb; the subject سپینود follows the verb because of poetic inversion. The coordinating و links the next noun phrase; بهرام را uses را to mark Bahram as a definite/emphatic object of the seeing.
segments.s638-g14: This NP is appositive: مران is a proper name, followed by مرد ('man') and postpositive epithets بی‌باک ('fearless') and خودکام ('self-willed/autocratic'), with را again marking the whole NP as definite/object-emphatic. Adjectival epithets typically follow the noun in classical Persian, and را links this NP back to the seeing verb.
segments.s638-g15: به دختر چنین گفت is 'he said thus to the maiden': به + NP marks the indirect object/recipient, چنین 'thus' qualifies the manner, and گفت is the past tense of گفتن 'to say' introducing direct speech. کای is the vocative 'O', addressing the addressee directly, and بدنژاد is an appellative 'low-born/ignoble' used as a direct address.
segments.s638-g16: This is the content of the speech introduced by که (here introducing a clause continuing the quoted admonition). چون تو 'such as you' (چون = 'like/as') + ز تخم بزرگان 'from the seed/lineage of the great' uses ز = 'from' (poetic). مباد is an optative/negative wish 'may it not be' (a common classical form expressing 'may it never be the case that...').
segments.s638-g33: بدانست is a past-tense verb meaning 'realized'/'perceived' and precedes the subject (poetic inversion): شنگل is the nominative (the one who realized). که introduces a subordinate content clause; او is the subject of that clause and راست گفت is the Clausial predicate (راست = 'true', گفت = past of 'to say' = 'said'). Overall: 'Shangal realized that he said the truth.'
segments.s638-g34: This is a nominal phrase (دلیری و گردی) followed by a predicative impersonal modal + passive/adjectival element: نشاید ('it is not fitting/should not') + نهفت (past participle/adj. 'hidden, concealed'). The coordinated nouns come before the predicate: 'It is not fitting that bravery and X be hidden.'
segments.s638-g35: بدو is a dative/clitic meaning 'to him' (dat. pronoun) placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and the subject name شنگل follows (poetic inversion). که introduces the reported content; فرزند را is the definite/marked object ('the child') with را.
segments.s638-g36: بیفگندم is a 1st-person form of افکندن (to cast/throw away) — here a volitional/future-like or subjunctive sense 'I will cast away' or 'I shall cast'. The rest lists coordinated objects: خویش (خوی + ش) = 'his/one's kin' (ش is the possessive suffix 'his'), و پیوند را adds 'and the bond' as another object; final را marks the definite object(s).
segments.s638-g37: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (used in comparisons), دیده = 'eye', and گرامی‌ترت is a comparative adjective 'dearer' with the 2nd-person suffix -ت marking the person involved ('you' = 'dearer (you)'). داشتـم is the past tense 'I had/held'. The clause means 'I held you dearer than my eye(s).'
segments.s638-g38: به سر = 'on (my) head'; بر here is a prepositional particle 'upon'; همی is an archaic/adverbial particle often giving durative/affirmative shade (here 'indeed' / part of idiomatic phrasing); افسرت is 'your helmet' (افسر/افسر + ت) meaning helmet; داشتم is again past 'I had'. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s638-g39: This clause is straightforward: ترا (written here split as ت + را) is the accusative/dative 'you'; دادم is past 'I gave'; آن را 'that (thing)' is the direct object; که introduces the relative clause 'which'; خود is the intensifier 'yourself'/'your own'; خواستی is 2nd-person past 'you wanted'. Word order: indirect object + verb + object + relative clause.
segments.s638-g40: This line is elliptic and inverted. مرا = 'my / me' (direct object), راستی = 'truth/honesty', بد = 'bad/evil' (qualifying how the honesty is regarded), ترا (here written split) = 'for you' (indirect object), and کاستی is the 2nd-person-past form (from کاستن) meaning 'you lessened/you diminished' — so the sense is 'You diminished my honesty / you made my honesty come to be seen as bad for you.' The verb (کاستی) is final and the clause shows classical inversion and suffixal morphology.
segments.s638-g49: چنان ... درست frames degree/emphasis: چنان (so/such) + noun phrase بچه شیر (lion‑cub) + بودی (past 'you were'), with درست as an adverb/postpositive intensifier meaning 'truly/exactly'. Word order is typical Persian: degree marker → noun phrase → copula → adverbial intensifier.
segments.s638-g50: که introduces a result/relative clause ('so ... that'). از خون دل is a figurative ablative 'out of / with the blood of (her) heart' (خون + دل as a genitive phrase). دایگانش is Dāyegān + -ش (possessive 'his wet‑nurse'), and بشست is the past tense 'washed' with the patient (him) left implicit.
segments.s638-g51: چو here = 'when'. دندان برآورد is a light verbal phrase 'put forth / bared his teeth' (برآورد used as past action). و links the clauses; شد + predicate (تیز چنگ) means 'his claw became sharp' (شد = become, adjective + noun follow the copula). The sequence is temporal: when X happened and Y became so.
segments.s638-g52: به marks the direction/recipient of the ensuing mental event. پروردگار here means 'the raiser/nurturer' (the one who fosters or has charge of him). آمدش is 'came to him/it' (the clitic -ش marks the indirect object). رای جنگ ('the counsel/plan of war') is the content that 'came' — a standard Persian construction 'to X came Y' meaning 'X was given/struck by Y (an idea/plan)'.
segments.s638-g53: بدو = 'to him' (prepositional/clitic form). گفت here is the reported‑speech verb 'said' but the provided sense list did not include that verbal sense (so it is marked below as unmatched). بهرام is the speaker's name following the verb (common in epic diction: indirect object → verb → subject). چون دانیم = 'how shall we consider/know' with چون = 'how' and دانیم = first‑person plural present/subjunctive of 'to know/consider'.
segments.s638-g54: This clause lists the possible judgments: بداندیش ('ill‑minded, malicious') و بدساز ('trouble‑maker / ill‑doer'). چون خوانیم repeats the interrogative 'how (shall we) call (him)?' — خوانیم here means 'we call/name' (one of its senses) rather than 'read', so the speaker is asking what label to apply.
segments.s638-g55: به رفتن is a gerundial/infinitival phrase 'in leaving / for going'. نباشد is a negative form of 'to be' here meaning 'there is not / there should be no'; the form is patterned as a negative present/subjunctive impersonal. م and را appear as separate tokens in the annotation but together mark the first‑person object ('me'); سرزنش = 'reproach/blame'. The whole means 'there should be no reproach of me for leaving.'
segments.s638-g56: نخواهی here functions as a negated future/optative 'you will not / do not (want to)'; in context it is an admonition 'do not (call)'. م and را again mark the first‑person object clitic ('me'). بددل 'inconstant/unfaithful' and بدکنش 'ill‑doer / badly‑behaved' are the labels the speaker asks not to be applied to him; the conjunction و links the two epithets.
segments.s638-g25: مرا آزمودی گه کارزار = 'You tested me on the battlefield.' مرا is the object 'me' (م + را, the object marker) placed before the verb for emphasis; آزمودی is 2nd-person singular past 'you tried/tested' (poetic past), and گه کارزار is an adverbial phrase 'on/at the occasion of battle' modifying آزمودی.
segments.s638-g26: چنانم که با باده و میگسار = 'I am such that (I am) with wine and a cupbearer.' چنانم is چنان + enclitic ـم 'I am such/so'; که introduces the clause (’that/just as’); با باده و میگسار is a prepositional phrase 'with wine and (a) cupbearer/wine‑companion' describing the manner.
segments.s638-g27: تو دانی که از هندوان صدهزار = 'You know that of (the) Indians a hundred thousand (there were).' تو دانی is 'you know' (present/gnomic); که introduces the content clause; از هندوان specifies 'of the Indians' and صدهزار gives the number 'one hundred thousand.'
segments.s638-g28: بود پیش من کمتر از یک سوار = 'there was, before me, fewer than a single rider.' بود is past 'was'; پیش من 'in front of me / before me' (i.e. in my presence); کمتر از introduces a comparison 'less/fewer than'; یک سوار 'one rider' is the standard of comparison.
segments.s638-g29: چو من باشم و نامور یار سی = 'When I am (present) and (there is) a famed band of thirty companions.' چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; من باشم uses باشم (subjunctive/present form of 'to be') to mark the temporal/hypothetical 'when I am'; و links to نامور یار سی 'a renowned/celebrated band of thirty companions' (سی here numerically 'thirty' modifying یار).
segments.s638-g30: زره‌دار با خنجر پارسی = 'armored (men) with Persian daggers.' زره‌دار is an adjective/noun 'armored (warriors)'; با introduces the instrument/weapon 'with'; خنجر پارسی 'Persian dagger' specifies the weapon and origin.
segments.s638-g31: پر از خون کنم کشور هندوان = 'I will make the land of the Indians full of blood.' پر از خون (literally 'full of blood') is a predicative phrase; کنم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/poetic-future 'I will make/do' (used here to indicate the speaker's action), and کشور هندوان 'the country/land of the Indians' is the object/place affected.
segments.s638-g32: نمانم که باشد کسی با روان = 'There will remain none such that anyone is left with a soul.' نمانم is negated/negative‑form first person 'there will not remain / will not be left' (in classical/poetic Persian this impersonal sense often means 'none will remain'); که introduces the subordinate clause; باشد is subjunctive/present 'there be' (used impersonally), کسی 'anyone', با روان 'with a soul/living.'
segments.s638-g17: تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun placed up front for emphasis ('you' / poetic 'thou'). با is the preposition 'with' marking accompaniment. این فریبنده مرد دلیر is a noun phrase: این (demonstrative 'this/that') + فریبنده (adjective 'deceptive/treacherous') + مرد (head noun 'man') + دلیر (adjective 'brave') — adjectives appear around the noun in poetic word order and the whole phrase is the accompaniment of 'تو'.
segments.s638-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دریا is the place noun 'sea' governed by ز. گذشتی is the past 2nd-person singular of گذشتن ('you passed/crossed'), placed after its objects as usual in Persian. به here marks manner/result and introduces کردار شیر ('in the manner/behavior of a lion'); کردار ('deed, conduct') + شیر ('lion') together mean 'lion-like conduct' (the ezāfe/linking vowel is omitted in elevated/poetic style).
segments.s638-g19: که introduces a subordinate/content clause ('that' / 'so that'). بی‌آگهی من is a possessive-like phrase meaning 'without my knowledge' (بی‌آگهی 'unaware/without knowledge' with من indicating the possessor: 'my'). به ایران شوی places the locative phrase before the verb: به ایران ('into/to Iran') + شوی (2nd-person singular form of شدن, here optative/subjunctive-ish 'you may become/you may go'), so the clause expresses the feared/resultant action.
segments.s638-g20: ز is the preposition 'from' marking origin ('from heavenly ...'). مینوی خرم is a compact poetic description: مینوی ('heavenly, celestial') modifying خرم ('joyful, blissful'), together implying a paradisal or blissful condition. به ویران شوی repeats the pattern of goal/result: به + adjective/noun ویران ('to/descent into ruin') + شوی (you may become), meaning 'you would come to be ruined.'
segments.s638-g21: ببینی is a jussive/imperative form of دیدن ('see' / 'behold'), used here to call attention ('see now' / 'look'). کنون is the adverb 'now' modifying that verb. زخم ژوپین من is a noun phrase 'the wound of my ژوپین (gambeson/padded coat)': زخم (wound) + ژوپین (the garment) + من (possessive 'my' — possessor follows the noun in classical/poetic order). The ezāfe linking particle is omitted as common in poetry.
segments.s638-g22: چو here means 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly/unexpectedly'. رفتی is the past 2nd-person singular 'you left' (verb at sentence end). ز بالین من is a prepositional phrase 'from my bedside' (ز = 'from', بالین = 'bedside/pillow', من = 'my'), giving the point of departure.
segments.s638-g23: بدو is a dative/adverbial phrase meaning 'to him' (or 'unto him'); گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the sense list provided did not include the verb meaning 'said' so that sense is not matched by the provided options). بهرام is the speaker's name in nominative position. کای is a vocative particle 'O' (classical/polite vocative). بدنشان is a possessed noun 'their body/bodies' or 'their persons' used as an epithet in vocative address (the possessor -شان follows the noun).
segments.s638-g24: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. تاختی is the past 2nd-person singular of تاختن ('you charged/rushed/attacked') and carries the main action. باره is a classical/poetic adverb/noun meaning roughly 'on that occasion' or 'then' (here marking the instance of the attack); its exact nuance is archaic. چون is the comparative/simile 'as/like'. بیهشان is an old/rare form whose exact sense is obscure in the modern lexicon (an archaic lexical item or proper name form), so its meaning here is uncertain and likely local/poetic; it appears to be the object/target reference in the simile.

sentences.s638-s45: One went toward the dry land and another toward the water; they went off cheerful of heart and in great haste.
sentences.s638-s37: Bahram brought forth the hidden secret and again spoke the words of the Iranians.
sentences.s638-s38: what the deeds were and what the thoughts were — and how I was guided into this tale.
sentences.s638-s39: They took a few bites and rose up, and they adorned their speech with apologies.
sentences.s638-s40: The two kings, beloved and God‑worshipping, pledged their fidelity hand to hand.
sentences.s638-s21: You chose cruelty in place of fidelity; how can you prefer cruelty as the due for loyalty?
sentences.s638-s22: What can I say — that he was your son? In my mind he was a wise man.
sentences.s638-s23: Now that he has become a brave horseman, I suppose he has become a ruler.
sentences.s638-s24: When has a Persian heart been faithful? When it consents, its counsel is not steadfast.
sentences.s638-s41: From now on we will not turn our hearts away from truth; we will uproot crookedness at its root.
sentences.s638-s42: Let us remain faithful so that forever we may hear words from the lips of the wise.
sentences.s638-s43: He too bade Sepinoud farewell, and set warp and weft upon himself.
sentences.s638-s44: They lightly turned their backs on one another; they left hatred in their hearts.
sentences.s638-s29: I am the Shahanshah of Iran and Turan; I am the commander and the backing of the brave.
sentences.s638-s30: From now on I will repay you with kindness; I will pluck the head of your ill‑omened hound from its body.
sentences.s638-s31: I will keep you in Iran in the place of your father; likewise I will not trouble you on account of the realm's uproar.
sentences.s638-s32: That very daughter of yours was the lamp of the East; she, like a helmet, was upon the heads of the ladies.
sentences.s638-s33: From his words there remained a strange astonishment; he lifted the shāreh from the Indian's head.
sentences.s638-s34: He struck his steed and, before so great an army, came forward to beg pardon near the king.
sentences.s638-s35: He joyfully took the emperor in his arms, and from those words accepted pardon.
sentences.s638-s36: At the sight of Bahram he became joyful; he arranged the banquet and brought a cup.
sentences.s638-s1: A horseman from Kannauj, one of the raiders, rode off to inform King Taft.
sentences.s638-s2: That Barzū and the Iranians have gone — they have taken that very daughter of the king.
sentences.s638-s3: Shangal heard this news from Nik‑khāh; like fire he came from the hunting‑ground.
sentences.s638-s4: He marshaled all his troops; then King Bahram put the army in motion.
sentences.s638-s5: Thus he reached up to the sea, and Sepinud saw Bahram the hero.
sentences.s638-s6: Sorrow arose, and it put the sea into wrath; from that side of the sea, when he turned his eye...
sentences.s638-s7: Sepinud saw him, and (he) saw Bahram — Maran, the fearless, self-willed man.
sentences.s638-s8: To the maiden he said thus: 'O low-born one, may it never be that you are of the seed of the great.'
sentences.s638-s17: Shangal realized that he had spoken the truth; courage and boldness should not be hidden.
sentences.s638-s18: Shangal said to him, 'I will cast away the child and his kin and the bond.'
sentences.s638-s19: I held you dearer than my own eyes; I had your helmet upon my head.
sentences.s638-s20: I gave you that which you yourself wanted; you have turned my honesty into badness — you diminished it.
sentences.s638-s25: You were such a true lion‑cub that his wet‑nurse washed him with the blood of her heart.
sentences.s638-s26: When he bared his teeth and his claws became sharp, the counsel of war came to his raiser.
sentences.s638-s27: Bahram said to him: 'How shall we judge him — (as) malicious and trouble‑making — and what shall we call him?'
sentences.s638-s28: There will be no reproach against me for going; do not call me inconstant and ill‑behaved.
sentences.s638-s13: You have tested me in battle; I am such as when I am with wine and a cupbearer.
sentences.s638-s14: You know that of the Indians there were a hundred thousand; before me they amounted to less than a single rider.
sentences.s638-s15: When I am present — and (when) a famed company of thirty companions (is) — armored men with Persian daggers, ...
sentences.s638-s16: I will fill the land of the Indians with blood; there will be none left with a living soul.
sentences.s638-s9: You, together with that treacherous brave man, crossed the sea and bore yourself like a lion.
sentences.s638-s10: That, unaware of me, you may come into Iran; from heavenly joy you may be reduced to desolation.
sentences.s638-s11: Now behold the wound in my gambeson, for you suddenly left my bedside.
sentences.s638-s12: To him Bahram said, 'O — of base body — why did you charge upon them as you did?'
